text: Upon entry into the abdomen, the hepatic flexure of the colon is observed to be in close proximity to the gallbladder.
The surgeon clears away surrounding tissue at an accelerated speed, revealing the absence of a clear plane between the gallbladder and the hepatic flexure of the colon.
The colon is visibly adhered to the liver, with no clear separation between the colon and the gallbladder.
The surgeon prepares to transect the gallbladder, a maneuver used in cholecystectomies when anatomical clarity is lacking.
The interior of the gallbladder is visualized, providing guidance for the ongoing dissection.
The surgeon identifies a point midway between the colon and the portal structures as the safest location for transection, preparing to separate these anatomical areas.
The surgeon dissects the gallbladder free from the liver fossa, despite not encountering the gallbladder lumen.
The surgeon transects the structure a second time, revealing the gallbladder with thickened walls and a narrow lumen filled with dark biliary sludge.
The surgeon continues dissection, revealing a narrowing that indicates the transition from the gallbladder to the cystic duct.
The surgeon makes a small incision in the cystic duct and inserts a balloon tip cholangiogram catheter into the duct.
The cholangiogram reveals the catheter positioned in a short cystic duct, with the balloon tip visible within the common bile duct.
The left and right hepatic ducts are visible, with contrast material flowing into the duodenum, indicating successful cholangiography.
The surgeon shifts focus to the gallbladder fundus and proceeds to remove a small segment of the colon, using robotic instruments to carefully excise the tissue.
The surgeon positions an endogia stapler across the side wall of the colon, preparing to transect the tissue.
The surgeon dissects the remaining colon and gallbladder fundus free from the liver, separating these structures from the hepatic tissue.
During the dissection, the surgeon encounters a large gallstone within the gallbladder, visible amidst the surrounding tissue.
The gallstone is placed aside for later removal along with the other specimens.
The extracted specimen shows the colon and gallbladder adhered together, with a visible fistula tract connecting the two structures.
Following the resection of the gallbladder fundus, the surgeon focuses on the cystic duct, preparing for its identification and dissection.
The surgeon observes that the encountered gallstone is too large to be secured with a five millimeter clip.
A ten millimeter polymer clip is applied through the umbilical extraction site to secure the structure, addressing the large size noted earlier.
The surgeon resects the remaining cystic duct and gallbladder infundibulum, completing the surgical procedure.
